authoritative_sources.title: DCC regulations
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DCC is California's official cannabis regulator and this page establishes the authoritative public regulatory baseline for commercial cannabis activity under MAUCRSA and Title 4 Division 19.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Lane-wide cannabis control framing and prerequisite review

authoritative_sources.title: Medicinal and Adult-Use Commercial Cannabis Regulations
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official DCC regulations document is a direct source for commercial cannabis inventory, transport, transfer-manifest, and recordkeeping obligations used by licensees.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cannabis transfer, movement, and recordkeeping review

authoritative_sources.title: Cannabis manufacturer: self-inspection checklist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This DCC-issued checklist is an official workflow reference for inventory management, track-and-trace, transfer-manifest creation, documentation readiness, and inspection evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cannabis inventory evidence and inspection-readiness review

authoritative_sources.title: Tracking in CTS
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OLCC is an official cannabis regulator and this page directly defines daily reconciliation, timely reporting, transfer recording, and training expectations for tracked medical marijuana facilities.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cannabis intake, reconciliation, and day-to-day inventory maintenance

authoritative_sources.title: Product Transfers
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This OLCC page is an authoritative public source for incoming and outgoing external transfer manifests, patient-sale boundaries, and inventory transfer request handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cannabis transfer and manifest exception handling

authoritative_sources.title: 21 CFR 1304.11 - Inventory requirements
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 21 CFR 1304.11 is the primary federal public baseline for controlled-substance initial and biennial inventories and count discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Controlled-substance inventory baseline and count review

authoritative_sources.title: 21 CFR 1304.21 - General requirements
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 21 CFR 1304.21 is the authoritative federal recordkeeping baseline for complete, accurate, and current controlled-substance records of receipt, sale, delivery, disposal, and other handling.

authoritative_sources.title: About the CSOS Program
authoritative_sources.publisher: Drug Enforcement Administration Diversion Control Division
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DEA's CSOS page is the official workflow reference for secure electronic controlled-substance orders, digital certificates, and Form 222 replacement mechanics.

authoritative_sources.title: ARCOS
authoritative_sources.publisher: Drug Enforcement Administration Diversion Control Division
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DEA's ARCOS page is the authoritative public workflow reference for the automated reporting system that monitors controlled-substance movement from manufacture through distribution to dispensing.

authoritative_sources.title: 21 CFR 1301.74 - Other security controls for non-practitioners; narcotic treatment programs and compounders for narcotic treatment programs
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 21 CFR 1301.74 is the federal public rule framing suspicious-order monitoring and related security controls for non-practitioner registrants.

authoritative_sources.title: Suspicious Orders Report System (SORS)
authoritative_sources.publisher: Drug Enforcement Administration Diversion Control Division
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DEA's SORS page is the official workflow reference for the centralized suspicious-order reporting database, covered registrant scope, and suspicious-order definitions under the SUPPORT Act.
used_for: when the lane must route to compliance rather than conclude the case

authoritative_sources.title: Theft/Loss Reporting
authoritative_sources.publisher: Drug Enforcement Administration Diversion Control Division
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DEA's Theft/Loss Reporting page is the official workflow reference for DEA Form 106, one-business-day notification, TLR submission, and significant-loss handling.

workflow_stages.name: Controlled inventory intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm jurisdiction, license or registrant class, product type, current system of record, and authority map before any availability or replenishment judgment is made.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Availability and replenishment assessment
workflow_stages.description: Compare package-tag, lot, count, receipt, hold, vendor, and ordering evidence to decide whether the requested inventory action is supportable.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Vendor, transfer, and movement action management
workflow_stages.description: Package bounded replenishment, vendor-follow-up, transfer-manifest, and logistics recommendations while preserving ordering, release, and substitution boundaries.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception escalation for shortages, discrepancies, suspicious orders, and losses
workflow_stages.description: Stop or escalate when shortages, incomplete traceability, suspicious-order signals, or significant-loss indicators make the answer depend on compliance, security, or licensed authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: state cannabis license records, facility identifiers, and jurisdiction context
DEA registration details, registrant class, controlled-substance schedule, and ordering eligibility records
state track-and-trace entries, package tags, UID values, transfer manifests, patient-sale records, and inventory adjustments
vault, warehouse, dispensary, or secure-storage counts with lot, batch, serial, or NDC-linked evidence
purchase requests, purchase orders, vendor acknowledgements, lead-time updates, CSOS certificate data, and DEA Form 222 references
quality-release or testing records, certificates of analysis, quarantine holds, and destruction or reverse-distribution documentation
discrepancy investigations, suspicious-order packages, ARCOS references, theft or loss notes, and DEA Form 106 preparation packets
availability summaries, procurement recommendations, and shortage or escalation notes produced by the lane
systems_of_record: state cannabis track-and-trace system such as CCTT or CTS
vault, warehouse, inventory, or point-of-sale system
quality management or laboratory information system
document repository for SOPs, approvals, and discrepancy investigations

allowed: classify requests as in-scope, blocked, or out-of-scope for controlled inventory readiness
summarize supply, shortage, manifest, count, vendor, and reporting dependencies with explicit evidence references
prepare bounded procurement, replenishment, and escalation packages without implying final regulatory or licensed authority
escalate: substitution, release, destruction, diversion, or legal-interpretation decisions requiring compliance, security, licensed, or legal authority
suspected suspicious orders or significant loss conditions that require formal reporting ownership
conflicting public and tenant policies on movement, release, discrepancy treatment, or reporting posture
refuse: bypass track-and-trace, manifest, CSOS, DEA Form 222, or reporting controls to preserve speed or service levels
represent unreconciled or held inventory as available for movement or dispensing
treat the industry label as cosmetic and collapse regulated inventory into generic warehouse advice

domain_failure_modes: Inventory is treated as available even though package-tag history, manifest completion, or controlled-substance receipt records are incomplete.
A shortage is answered with a generic substitution suggestion that crosses licensed, legal, or clinical authority boundaries.
Discrepancies, suspicious-order signals, or significant-loss indicators are treated as routine inventory noise instead of compliance or security escalations.
Cannabis transfer rules or DEA ordering rules are ignored because the lane collapses into generic procurement behavior.
State cannabis guidance and DEA or tenant policy differences are blended into a confident answer without noting the regulatory regime that governs the facility.
The lane overreaches by filing authoritative reports or approving release, destruction, or procurement actions that belong to adjacent specialists.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Required supply is unavailable or short
Vendor or substitution decision exceeds documented authority
Traceability or inventory evidence is incomplete

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State cannabis transfer, manifest, and transport rules vary materially by jurisdiction and sometimes by license type
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack uses California and Oregon official sources as named-industry baselines, but runtime must retrieve the actual state and license program in force for the tenant before finalizing manifest-sensitive guidance.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Controlled-substance ordering, suspicious-order, and reporting duties vary by DEA registrant class and product schedule
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public DEA baseline applies broadly, but runtime must know whether the tenant acts as manufacturer, distributor, pharmacy, hospital, practitioner, or other registrant before making ordering or reporting-sensitive recommendations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Federal and state treatment of cannabis may point to different legal regimes for the same operator
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This pack avoids resolving legal conflicts and instead requires runtime retrieval of the operator's jurisdiction, license posture, and legal or compliance escalation path whenever the answer depends on legal interpretation.

notes: The source mix covers named-industry operating guidance, workflow-system references, and evidence or control standards for cannabis and controlled-substance inventory work.

No unresolved ambiguity remains blocking for a validated spec pack because the package explicitly requires tenant retrieval of jurisdiction, license, registrant class, and authority maps before final action.
